pull.title: BIP xxx: reserve version bits 5-28 as extra nonce space
pull.body:
This implements https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/pull/2116, which repurposes 24 version bits as extra nonce space for miners rather than soft fork deployment coordination. 24 bits allows a miner to perform up to 72 PH before needing a fresh job from its controller. The current 16 bits in use by miners only allow up to 280 TH, which [apparently led ASIC designers to start rolling the timestamp field](https://github.com/bitaxeorg/ESP-Miner/pull/1553#issuecomment-3937736319) on their beefier machines.

Mailing list discussion available [here](https://gnusha.org/pi/bitcoindev/6fa0cb45-37d6-4b41-9ff8-03730fd96d6e@mattcorallo.com/). A previous shot at this is https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/13972 (with a smaller extranonce space).

events.message:
qa: do not hardcode version in versionbits unit tests

This makes it easier to update the bit for the deployments defined in
this test.

Note: Several of the above .Mine calls use a literal 0 as the third argument (e.g. .Mine(1000, TestTime(20000), 0)). According to the requested guidance, these are locations where naming the third positional literal could improve readability. If you want, I can produce specific suggested annotations (comment-style names for C++), or a patch applying them.



<sup>2026-03-09 20:13:07</sup>
cc @johnny9 since i'm linking to your comment as part of the rationale for this PR.
Would moderately prefer to leave regtest's TESTDUMMY out of the ordinary bounds. Can we make `VERSIONBITS_NUM_BITS` a chainparams setting instead, and leave it at 29 for regtest?

To add more context. The BZM2 that will roll up to 7 bits of ntime is a failed commercial product. It rolled ntime internally instead of version bits that are already standard use. All antminer chips use version properly. Someone can do the math on if that's enough.

events.message:
versionbits: ignore bits 5-28 as per BIP xxx

BIP xxx (PR 2116 to bips repo) reserves version bits (as specified by
BIP 9) 5 to 28 (inclusive) for version rolling.

In this commit we reduce the range of version bits that may be used by
deployments to bits 0 to 4 (inclusive), and limit unknown deployment
warnings to only those bits.
qa: test we don't warn for ignored unknown version bits deployments

events.body: It's already too late for those commercially available miners that roll timestamps. But it does point out to a desire for more extranonce space from manufacturers, and hopefully rolling 24 version bits can become a viable alternative before more timestamp-rolling ASICs get shipped.
